abstract: This article examines how professional fact-checkers in Europe rhetorically defend their funding and collaborative ties to major platform companies such as Meta, Alphabet, and ByteDance. Based on 12 qualitative interviews with fact-checkers from 12 European countries, the authors apply rhetorical apologia theory to identify recurring justificatory arguments. They find that fact-checkers rely on four modes of differentiation and three modes of transcendence to assert editorial independence and legitimise platform partnerships while acknowledging their risks. The study illuminates how fact-checkers negotiate institutional dependencies and uphold epistemic authority amid criticism from journalists, far-right actors, and even platform insiders.
key_claims: European fact-checkers adopt predominantly defensive rhetoric when discussing platform partnerships, drawing on transformative apologia strategies of differentiation and transcendence.

Fact-checkers engage in rhetorical boundary work that simultaneously acknowledges risks of platform dependence and asserts pragmatic necessity, mirroring traditional journalism's 'wall' metaphors of autonomy.

Boundaries fact-checkers draw between themselves and platform companies are not uniform across Europe — some accept funding from actors (e.g., TikTok, the state) that others reject, reflecting the still-emergent state of the fact-checking field.
Defensive rhetoric is not only about external legitimacy but also reflects internal struggles over the normative foundations and professional identity of fact-checking.
methods: 12 semi-structured qualitative interviews with professional fact-checkers from 12 different fact-checking organisations across 12 European countries (Czech Republic, Estonia, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Latvia, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Slovakia, Spain, Sweden), conducted November 2023–March 2024.
Interviews averaged 71 minutes and were structured around six themes including organisational structure, audience, societal role, editorial independence, challenges, and platform companies.

Four-phase qualitative coding in NVivo: inductive theme identification, coding, theoretical application of Ware and Linkugel's (1973) apologia framework, and grouping of quotes by rhetorical defence type.
Anonymisation of all interviewees and organisational details to mitigate risks to partnerships and prevent targeted harassment.
findings: Four modes of differentiation identified: (D1) from platform companies (asserting editorial independence), (D2) between platform companies (e.g., defending Meta partnerships by rejecting TikTok/ByteDance), (D3) within platform companies (distinguishing well-intentioned employees from corporate motives), and (D4) from the state (framing platform funding as less compromising than government funding).

Three modes of transcendence identified: (T1) invoking a counterfactual worse reality without fact-checking, (T2) appealing to broad alliances needed against disinformation, and (T3) framing collaboration as a way to improve platforms from within.
All 12 organisations received funding from at least one major platform company between 2021 and early 2024, most commonly Meta (9), Alphabet (8), ByteDance (2), Twitter (2), and Spotify (2).
Fact-checkers report frequent accusations of censorship, bias, and collusion — particularly from far-right actors — which often cite platform funding as 'evidence' of wrongdoing.
Disagreement among European fact-checkers about acceptable funders (e.g., TikTok, state) reveals that rhetorical boundaries are inconsistent and contested across the field.
contributions: First application of rhetorical apologia theory to fact-checkers' defences of platform partnerships, complementing prior boundary-work scholarship.
Provides a typology (four differentiation + three transcendence modes) for analysing fact-checkers' justificatory rhetoric.
Addresses the underexamined European context of fact-checking, which has been studied less than North America despite hosting more fact-checking institutions.
Extends Graves et al.'s (2023) analysis of internal boundaries by focusing on external boundaries between fact-checkers and platform companies, and between different platform actors.
Adapts apologia theory from its original application (strategic public defences of prior actions) to ongoing institutional relationships in qualitative interview settings.
framing: The paper situates itself at the intersection of fact-checking studies, journalism studies on boundary work and epistemic authority (Graves, Singer, Schudson, Karlsson and Örnebring), and rhetorical apologia theory (Ware and Linkugel). It positions fact-checking as an emergent epistemic institution whose legitimacy is negotiated rhetorically rather than through formal credentialing, especially in the contested terrain of platform dependency.
